name: Bacteroides
Comments:
It is the most common enterotype in developed countries. It is dominated by species like Bacteroides, Phocaeicola, or Parabacteroides and often observed in individuals who eat mostly animal-based foods & with lower consumption of plant-based foods.
The Bacteroides enterotype tends to have higher levels of Bifidobacterium species, and compared to other enterotypes, has the highest potential to produce vitamin B12 and biotin (vitamin B7).
Studies have shown that gut types respond differently to dietary interventions. While fiber-rich foods are beneficial for everyone, if trying to lose weight, the Bacteroides enterotype may not benefit much from a fiber-rich diet.

There are two subtypes in the Bacteroides enterotype, named Bact1 and Bact2. 
The Bact2 subtype has three main characteristics in terms of microbiome composition: 1) top species include Bacteroides fragilis, Phocaeicola vulgatus, and Parabacteroides distasonis; 2) lower diversity; and 3) very low levels of Faecalibacterium prausnitzii. This subtype is more often seen in individuals with obesity, insulin resistance, or inflammatory conditions such as IBD and multiple sclerosis.

name: Prevotella
Comments:
This is an uncommon enterotype in developed countries. It is frequently dominated by Prevotella copri and is often observed in individuals that eat a lot of plant fiber-rich foods.
Different strains of P. copri have different food preferences; while some strains may be very good at digesting fibers in plant-based foods, others may not be so good. This variable nature of P. copri may explain the different and sometimes contradictory health associations that studies have found for this enterotype. Prevotella species have been positively associated with the production of beneficial SCFAs, improved glucose metabolism, anti-inflammatory effects, effective weight loss or maintenance when on a high-fiber diet (especially with fibers found in cereal grains), and lower cholesterol.
However some studies have also associated P. copri with, inflammatory conditions, and insulin resistance and glucose intolerance.
In any case, the Prevotella enterotype is often seen in people with non-Western and/or fiber-rich diets heavy in plant-based foods. This enterotype has the highest potential to digest cellulose.

name: Ruminococcaceae
Comments:
In Western countries, the Ruminococcaceae enterotype is less common than the Prevotella and Bacteroides enterotypes. It is dominated by species like Ruminococcus, Ruminiclostridium, and Faecalibacterium, which are good at breaking down fiber from carbohydrates to produce beneficial SCFAs. Some species also feed on acetate produced by other bacteria, and transform it into butyrate.
Not many studies have explored how the Ruminococcaceae enterotype responds to dietary or supplement interventions. However, due to its ability to break down carbohydrates, the Ruminococcaceae enterotype could make it easy to gain weight if eating too many carbs, due to increased nutrient availability and absorption.

name: Lachnospiraceae
Comments: Found in 35% of adults, this is one of the most common enterotype in Western countries. It is often dominated by species like Anaerostipes, Blautia, and Roseburia, which are good at digesting plant fibers such as starch, inulin and pectin. From these, Lachnospiraceae produce beneficial SCFAs like butyrate. That said, the ability to digest fiber and produce SCFAs varies depending on the species.

name: Bifidobacterium
Comments:
As the name suggests, this enterotype is dominated by beneficial Bifidobacterium species. It is usually observed in many toddlers and some children. It is hence is characterized as an immature gut community and is rarely found in adults. Common species that dominate this gut type are Bifidobacterium adolescentis and Bifidobacterium catenulatum. Compared to the Bacteroides and Prevotella enterotypes, the Bifidobacterium gut type has lower diversity. As this enterotype is considered to be more immature, it may not be able to properly digest complex carbohydrates typical of an adult diet. It is unclear what this means for adults.
This enterotype is better than other ones at producing branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs), which are essential for building protein in muscle cells.
An adult with a Bifidobacterium enterotype should try to diversify their gut microbiome. Even if Bifidobacterium are beneficial bacteria, it is not ideal that they dominate the gut.

name: Other
Comments: Instead of being dominated by Bacteroides, Prevotella, Ruminococcaceae, Lachnospiraceae, or Bifidobacterium, the ‘Other’ enterotype has higher levels of a variety of species that don’t fit in the main categories. Having this enterotype is not a bad thing; in fact, its high diversity may be beneficial for gut health and is observed in a relatively high percentage of the population.

name: Enterobacteriaceae
Comments:
This is a rare gut type that only a couple of studies have identified in Asian populations. It has high levels of potentialy pathogenic bacteria such as Escherichia, Klebsiella, Salmonella, Citrobacter, Enterobacter and Raoultella, which could be a temporary state.
One of the studies reported that compared to individuals with a Bacteroides or Prevotella enterotypes, those with an Enterobacteriaceae enterotype ate a higher amount of red meat and had quicker transit times. 
It’s also possible for this enterotype to be the result of a recent gut infection or antibiotic treatment. Some of the top antibiotic-resistant bacteria are species of Escherichia and Klebsiella. If this is the case, it should be possible for this enterotype to shift to a different composition after recovery, potentially helped by probiotics or other supplements.

name: Type 1a
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 1 - Bifidobacterium-dominated. Subtype A: medium or high Bifidobacterium. 
Ideally, Bifidobacterium species are present in the infant’s gut from birth onward, although we would expect higher levels in infants and children under the age of three. Infant Enterotype 1 is linked to a vaginal birth with few medical interventions and breastfeeding. Breastfeeding has a significant impact on the development of Bifidobacterium. Within a couple of weeks of breastfeeding, a baby born via C-section can have comparable levels of Bifidobacterium as a baby born vaginally. As long as the infant is breastfed, Bifidobacterium will generally remain high in their gut during their first 6 months of life. This is important because low levels of Bifidobacterium in babies are linked to chronic diseases, including autoimmunity, asthma, and obesity.

name: Type 1b
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 1 - Bifidobacterium-dominated. Subtype B: low Bifidobacterium. 
Ideally, Bifidobacterium species are present in the infant’s gut from birth onward, although we would expect higher levels in infants and children under the age of three. Infant Enterotype 1 is linked to a vaginal birth with few medical interventions and breastfeeding. Breastfeeding has a significant impact on the development of Bifidobacterium. Within a couple of weeks of breastfeeding, a baby born via C-section can have comparable levels of Bifidobacterium as a baby born vaginally. As long as the infant is breastfed, Bifidobacterium will generally remain high in their gut during their first 6 months of life. This is important because low levels of Bifidobacterium in babies are linked to chronic diseases, including autoimmunity, asthma, and obesity.

name: Type 2a
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 2 - Bacteroidaceae-dominated. Subtype A: medium or high Bifidobacterium. 
Bacteroidaceae are generally beneficial gut bacteria and they’re found in almost all individuals. Some species of Bacteroidaceae can degrade some of the sugars in breastmilk (HMOs) but they may not be as efficient as Bifidobacterium in breaking down all types of HMOs. This enterotype is linked to vaginal births, and may help prepare the infant’s gut for the introduction of solid food. Together with Bifidobacterium, Bacteroidaceae play a major role in the early gut microbiome development. It is important to note that Bacteroides are absent in almost all infants born by C-section delivery and are particularly affected by antibiotics given to the mom either before or during labor. If and when possible, we advocate for an antibiotic-free vaginal birth so that the infant’s gut can shelter the best bacteria possible.

name: Type 2b
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 2 - Bacteroidaceae-dominated. Subtype B: low Bifidobacterium. 
Bacteroidaceae are generally beneficial gut bacteria and they’re found in almost all individuals. Some species of Bacteroidaceae can degrade some of the sugars in breastmilk (HMOs) but they may not be as efficient as Bifidobacterium in breaking down all types of HMOs. This enterotype is linked to vaginal births, and may help prepare the infant’s gut for the introduction of solid food. Together with Bifidobacterium, Bacteroidaceae play a major role in the early gut microbiome development. It is important to note that Bacteroides are absent in almost all infants born by C-section delivery and are particularly affected by antibiotics given to the mom either before or during labor. If and when possible, we advocate for an antibiotic-free vaginal birth so that the infant’s gut can shelter the best bacteria possible.

name: Type 3a
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 3 - Enterobacteriaceae-dominated. Subtype A: medium or high Bifidobacterium. 
Enterobacteriaceae are bacteria found almost everywhere in nature, including the infant’s gut. Some species are beneficial and even desired in the newborn gut. However, at high enough levels, some Enterobacteriaceae can cause serious problems due to their pro-inflammatory properties. For example, Klebsiella species have been associated with the development of a higher incidence of respiratory infections later in life. Many Enterobacteriaceae species often carry genes related to antibiotic resistance that allow them to survive exposure to antibiotics, and are particularly dangerous in premature babies. Because Enterobacteriaceae are common members of the hospital environment, this enterotype is frequently seen in C-section born babies, vaginal hospital births, and babies born prematurely, who often have to spend extensive time in the hospital.

name: Type 3b
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 3 - Enterobacteriaceae-dominated. Subtype B: low Bifidobacterium. 
Enterobacteriaceae are bacteria found almost everywhere in nature, including the infant’s gut. Some species are beneficial and even desired in the newborn gut. However, at high enough levels, some Enterobacteriaceae can cause serious problems due to their pro-inflammatory properties. For example, Klebsiella species have been associated with the development of a higher incidence of respiratory infections later in life. Many Enterobacteriaceae species often carry genes related to antibiotic resistance that allow them to survive exposure to antibiotics, and are particularly dangerous in premature babies. Because Enterobacteriaceae are common members of the hospital environment, this enterotype is frequently seen in C-section born babies, vaginal hospital births, and babies born prematurely, who often have to spend extensive time in the hospital.

name: Type 4a
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 4 - Enterococcus-dominated. Subtype A: medium or high Bifidobacterium. 
Some species of Enterococcus can both beneficial and pathogenic. Either way, if this bacteria dominates the infant’s gut, it’s likely not ideal. This enterotype is linked to both vaginal and C-section births. The presence of Enterococcus faecalis in babies has been associated with respiratory infections, and this bacteria is more likely than others to carry genes related to antibiotic resistance. When these pathogenic species dominate the infant’s microbiome, you may want to consider ways of decreasing E. faecalis levels and increasing the levels of beneficial Bifidobacterium. This is not a very common gut type.

name: Type 4b
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 4 - Enterococcus-dominated. Subtype B: low Bifidobacterium. 
Some species of Enterococcus can both beneficial and pathogenic. Either way, if this bacteria dominates the infant’s gut, it’s likely not ideal. This enterotype is linked to both vaginal and C-section births. The presence of Enterococcus faecalis in babies has been associated with respiratory infections, and this bacteria is more likely than others to carry genes related to antibiotic resistance. When these pathogenic species dominate the infant’s microbiome, you may want to consider ways of decreasing E. faecalis levels and increasing the levels of beneficial Bifidobacterium. This is not a very common gut type.

name: Type 5a
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 5 - Streptococcus-dominated. Subtype A: medium or high Bifidobacterium. 
Streptococci are mostly found in the mouth, stomach, and small intestine of healthy individuals. They can be a small part of a healthy early-life microbiome community but are not ideal to have as a dominant infant enterotype. Some Streptococcus species may be associated with increased risk for eczema and type I diabetes. This is not a very common gut type.

name: Type 5b
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 5 - Streptococcus-dominated. Subtype B: low Bifidobacterium. 
Streptococci are mostly found in the mouth, stomach, and small intestine of healthy individuals. They can be a small part of a healthy early-life microbiome community but are not ideal to have as a dominant infant enterotype. Some Streptococcus species may be associated with increased risk for eczema and type I diabetes. This is not a very common gut type.

name: Type 6a
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 6 - Staphylococcus-dominated. Subtype A: medium or high Bifidobacterium. 
Staphylococcus is mostly found on the skin and is not adapted to live in the infant or adult gut. But these bacteria are sometimes found to colonize the gut of infants born through C-section delivery. It’s important to note that we expect Staphylococcus to decrease quickly throughout the first months of life as it is outcompeted by other species that are better adapted to living in the gut. Colonization of newborn babies with Staphylococcus aureus may increase the risk of infection in early life, and the presence of S. aureus in the nose of children has been associated with asthma. This is not a very common gut type.

name: Type 6b
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 6 - Staphylococcus-dominated. Subtype B: low Bifidobacterium. 
Staphylococcus is mostly found on the skin and is not adapted to live in the infant or adult gut. But these bacteria are sometimes found to colonize the gut of infants born through C-section delivery. It’s important to note that we expect Staphylococcus to decrease quickly throughout the first months of life as it is outcompeted by other species that are better adapted to living in the gut. Colonization of newborn babies with Staphylococcus aureus may increase the risk of infection in early life, and the presence of S. aureus in the nose of children has been associated with asthma. This is not a very common gut type.

name: Type 7a
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 7 - Other. Subtype A: medium or high Bifidobacterium. 
Sometimes an infant's gut microbiome does not fit into any of the six Infant Enterotypes defined. If an infant is type 7, that means that a high percentage of the bacteria present in their gut are species that we don’t normally see dominating an infant’s gut. Before 6 months, this is quite rare and is usually linked to a C-section birth, vaginal birth with medical interventions, or formula-feeding. If the infant is 6 months or older and Type 7, don’t be alarmed. This Infant Enterotype is a sign that the microbiome has started to diversify. It may be normal if the infant has started solids or weaned from breastmilk, which can take place between 6-9 months.

name: Type 7b
Comments:
Infant Enterotype 7 - Other. Subtype B: low Bifidobacterium. 
Sometimes an infant's gut microbiome does not fit into any of the six Infant Enterotypes defined. If an infant is type 7, that means that a high percentage of the bacteria present in their gut are species that we don’t normally see dominating an infant’s gut. Before 6 months, this is quite rare and is usually linked to a C-section birth, vaginal birth with medical interventions, or formula-feeding. If the infant is 6 months or older and Type 7, don’t be alarmed. This Infant Enterotype is a sign that the microbiome has started to diversify. It may be normal if the infant has started solids or weaned from breastmilk, which can take place between 6-9 months.
